Alright, you're back. Let's pick up right where we left off in the last lesson, which was getting ready to raise our prospect, Bill, his level of certainty for the second 10, using my absolutely unequivocally world famous Forrest Gump patent. However, before we actually do that or unleash that, we first need to set up the correct frame. See, there are actually two powerful language patterns that work hand in hand with one another to actually create the Forrest Gump patent. Let me take you through them one at a time, starting with Bill's response to your follow-up presentation, which landed him at a nine on the certainty scale for the first 10. So we're going to create some context for you. So Bill said to you, Oh, you know, absolutely. I love the idea. Makes total sense to me. Just, you know, give you a really enthusiastic response, right? To what you then shot back saying, exactly. It really is an amazing opportunity. And bam, just like that, you've formally closed out your last pattern, which serves to lock down Bill's first 10. And now it's also going to act as the launching pad for the next pattern, your Forrest Gump patent, which we'll use to lock down your second 10. Now it starts with putting a slight pause between these two patterns, meaning the old pattern they just finished and the new one, this Forrest Gump patent, in order to de-emphasize the sudden shift in tonality you're about to make from one of absolute certainty. Meaning you are said, you know, absolutely really is a great idea, right? That was absolute certainty. And now in your new pattern, you're going to start with a tone of mystery and intrigue, as opposed to you going with full certainty into the next pattern. So watch, it will be certainty into certainty with no pause. You'd be like, exactly Bill. It really is a great idea. And now let me ask you another question. He's like, whoa, this is all related. Some guys are running a strategy on me to pressure me to buy, right? You want to knock that out right there. So you're going to place that pause between the two patterns and you had shift in tone to mystery and intrigue. So it's as if the pattern just kind of, oh, an idea just popped in my head. That's the best way to explain it. I think it kind of gets the point of course, right? Now again, we're going to use stocks here, stock market as our sample product. Again, it doesn't matter what we use here. This Forrest Gump patent, both parts of it can be used for any product, any good at any service in person over the phone. I mean, if it doesn't matter, and I will explain all of that in the exercises in the back end and the resource center. So don't worry. Just we'll use stocks. It's cool. It's fun. It's how it all started, but don't worry. It works just as good as the everything. All right. So we'll use our words here like exactly Bill. The stock really is a screaming buy, Daniel. And then you would pause now for a brief incident you were in in absolute certainty, right? And now you switch to your tonality of mystery and intrigue and you say, now, Bill, let me ask you another question. And now you switch once again to your money aside or hypothetical tonality. And you'd say, if I've been your broker for the last three or four years, making your money in a consistent basis, then you probably wouldn't be saying, let me think about it right now, Bill. You'd be saying, Jordan, pick me up a block of at least a few thousand shares and then you switch now to the reasonable man tonality and finish and say, am I right? Get that all said again. Say, Bill, let me ask you another question. If I've been your broker for the last three or four years, making your money in a consistent basis, then you probably wouldn't be saying, let me think about it right now. You'd be saying, pick me up a block of at least a few thousand shares. Am I right? That's how it sounds together, right? Now, what you'll find here is that with that one pattern, at least 95% of your prospects will come totally clean with you at this point and say something short and sweet like, yeah, well, then I would. Or you know, obviously, I mean, who wouldn't then, right? Or, yeah, well, yeah, that'd be a whole different thing. You get it? Whichever variation you hear at the end of the day, what they all boil down to is the same awesome reality. Namely, that your prospect has just admitted that it's trust or a lack thereof. That's not just a cornerstone issue, but the cornerstone issue for them. In other words, once your prospect openly admits they love your product as they have in this case, right? They were at nine or plus on the surface scale. It now significantly increases the importance of openly admitting that it's actually trust that's holding them back from buying. And if you hit that one step further, once they do indeed admit that, that it's actually a lack of trust that's holding them back, not that bogus objection they hit you with, you've now cut to the heart of what the straight line system is truly all about, which is pushing aside all the stalls and smoke streams that cause the average salesperson to plunge into that reprobating death spiral, so you can now get to the heart of what's really holding your prospect back, which is a lack of certainty to be the one, two, or all three of the three tens, or perhaps a really high action threshold, or the complete absence of pain. You get it? One of those five core elements. The objection now melts away because they themselves have admitted, yeah, it's actually trust. It's not that I need to think about it or call you back. It's just that they love the product. What's happening now is it's almost like a box forming, a logic box. And the prospect starts saying, wow, I just said I love this product. And now I've just admitted that I'm not buying because I don't trust the person, and they feel empowered. You know why? Because here's the deal. Every other salesperson just tries to come at it with that rapport breaking strategy of, well, tell me, Bill, what is it that's holding you back? Well, what do you have that I said? What do I need to say right now that will make you buy from here? Think about that. All it does is pushes Bill on the defensive, and you're done. That's why average salespeople have such low closing rates when they get hit with objections because they try to overcome them versus moving past them and getting to the heart of what influences really all about in a sales setting, namely, bring it down to what's real. Uncertainty for one, two, or all three of the three tens, or perhaps they just got a really high action threshold like my dad does. So you got to get them really, really certain and lower that action threshold. Or maybe it's just a total absence of pain. So they're in denial. So there's no impetus to act now. Makes sense? Powerful stuff, right? Now, remember, I said 95% will come clean. For those annoying 5% of the prospects who don't come clean, me, they reject your hypothesis that it's actually trust that's holding them back and not their bogus objection. Well, what you're going to do now is you'll come act with everything you've got. Now, when I say everything you've got, I don't mean you can say, well, Bill, let me ask you this. You need to tell me that if I knew you, you wouldn't be, don't do that. You're not going to come back and wait a second, idiot. Stop screwing or that's not going to get you. Anyway, they'll break the report and you'll be done. Instead, your voice is going to take on almost like a mocking tone, almost mixed with an incredulity. Like, you're basically calling him out in this bullshit, but in a way that will be kind and respectful and earn his respect most of all. So you'll say, wait a second, Bill. I mean, you need to tell me that if I put you into Union Carbide at 7 and took you out at 32 and then put you to US Steel at 16, see, I'm almost like there's a laughter I tell them. I took you out of 41 and put you to Facebook at 70 and took you out at 130. You'll be saying, Jordan, people used to few thousand shares right now on, so come on, Bill, really. And with that, if you do it that way, the other 5% will almost, I mean, it's like 0.001, 0.991, we'll say, yeah, well, then I would. They'll come clean, right? But the only difference there is many will have almost like this tone in their voice is a bit defensive that it wasn't that they are flip-flopping on their answer. It was your fault for flip-flopping on your question. As if that you didn't really tell them that in the first place. In other words, like, why did you ask that in the first place? Of course, I want to say that you get it so they get a little defensive because they got called out. Here's the deal. Well, that's not great to have to call someone out. In this case, it's pretty much wholly irrelevant because what's going to happen is that defensiveness is going to quickly melt away as you begin your next pattern. And you're now in this perfect poll position to close the sale, starting with the fact that you've successfully reframed what the sale's all about. You see, while Bill's original objection was, let me think about it, rather than acting like every other salesperson out there who asked them that dead-end question, like I just said, right? You know, press asking, what do you want to think about, Bill, right? You've took control of the sale and started picking the lock on Bill's buying strategy. Now, again, Bill, on the other hand, he'll be completely taken aback because you've come out of this entirely different angle, different matter that he's used to. You get it? In fact, you even really answered his objection before it came up. Case in point, his real objection is what? He doesn't know you and hence has no basis for trusting you. You get it, right? So you're able to bring that to the surface in a very elegant way. That's why I said Bill feels empowered, right? So he's sort of in this box, right? But the box is sort of a lot of loopholes because Bill could easily get out because he said, yeah, sure, I love the product, but I don't trust you. So he's got one wall in the box, the other wall is porous because all of the bill says, yeah, well, I don't trust you. That's why I'm not buying, right? So in order to close this box a little bit, what you have to do now is convince someone, in this case, Bill, who's only knowing it's maybe five or 10 minutes or so, right? I'm wondering, you're pitching the guy, right? Who's never met you in person, perhaps, who could even be the other side of the culture you are in these days, for that matter, the world, right? You have to convince this person to trust you at an incredibly high level in the next 60 seconds or so. As I said earlier, to an outside observer, that would seem like a really daunting task, right? But to us, we know differently because see with the forest gum pattern, we know how human beings are built, meaning that pendulum of trust, when it starts to swing, wham, it swings always, it swings violently, and all you have to do to get that pendulum of trust swinging from I don't trust you at all, to I totally trust you, is to execute an awesome forest gum pattern. So with that, I want to stop right now, take a short quiz, and come right back after that, and it'll actually take you to the second half, which is your actually, your forest gum pattern itself, and we'll rock on, all right?
